content.full_text:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Cupertino
Councilmernber Sandra {ames regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the'Act").I Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct
that has already taken place. Since you do not ask about a particular governmental
decision, we are rendering informal assistance.z
Farn PolrrrcAl- PRACTTcES CouvrrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
October 20,2004
Charles T. Kilian
Office of the City Attorney
City of Cupertino
20410 Town Center Lane, Suite 210
Cupertino, CA 9501 4-3255
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our FiIe No. I-04-189
Dear Mr. Kilian:
QUESTION
If Councilmember James accepts a salaried position with the Cupertino Chamber
of Commerce, will she be disqualified from participating in future land use and business
decisions regarding land and businesses owned by mernbers of the chamber of
commerce?
CONCLUSION
Not necessarily. If Councilmember James were to be employed by the local
chamber of commerce as executive director, the conflict-oFinterest provisions of the Act
require her disqualification in future land use and business decisions regarding Iand and
businesses owned by members of the chamber of commerce only if there was a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the chamber as a result or decision, or
if the decision had a personal financial effect on the council mernber.
' Government Code sections 81000 - 9l}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 831 14; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


File No. I-04-189
Page No. 2
FACTS
Sandra James is an elected city council member who is considering employment
with the local chamber of commerce in Cupertino as its salaried executive director. The
chamber of commerce is a private nonprofit orguization that collects money from dues
paylng members in order to promote the business climate in the City of Cupertino. Most
major businesses, including those which own large tracts of undeveloped and partially
developed land, me dues paying members of the chamber of commerce-
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused bytheir own financial interests or
the financial interests ofpersons who have supported them-" (Section 8l00lO).) Section
87100 prohibits anypublic official from making participating in making or otherwise
using his orher official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official
has a financial interest.
The general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public
official makes, participates in making, or otherwise uses his or her official position to
influence a govenrmental decision which has a reasonably foreseeable material financial
effect on one or more of his or her economic interests-
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analys,is for deciding whether
an official has a disqualiffing conflict of interest- Analysis ofthe following questions is
applied below. (Regulation I 8700.)
Questions One and Two: Is Ms. James considered a'public oflicial'and is she
making, participating in making, or influencing a governmentat decision?
The Act's corrflict-of-interest provisions apply only to public officials. "Public
official" is defind as "every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local
government agency ...." (Section 82048.) A city council rnember is a public official and is,
therefore, subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest rules.
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any murse of action, or enters into any contracfual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. @egulation 18702.1.) A public official
"participates in making a govefllmental decision" when voting on an issue that comes
before him or her in his or her official capacity.
A mernber of the city council makes governmental decisions anytime he or she
exercises discretion or judgment with regard to an issue that is brought before the official.


File No. I-04-189
Page No. 3
Question Three: What are the council member's economic interests - the possible
sources of a conflict of interest?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts arising from
economic interests. The economic interests from which conflicts may arise are defined
within section 87103 of the Act and regulations 18703 - 18703.5. The two implicated by
your request are:
(l) 
Any source of income, includingpromisod incomg which aggregates to
$500 or more within 12 months priof to the decision. (Section 87103(c);
regulation I 8703.3) and
(2) 
His or her personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those
of his or her immediate family- This is known as the "personal financial
effects" rule. (Section 87103; regulation 18703.5.)
Clearly, the chamber will be an economic interest of the cormcil member by virtue
of the salar5r received. However, your question is not whether Councilmemb€r James is
required to disqualiff herself where the chamber of commerce appears before her, but
rather will she be required to disqualifu herself when members of the chamber appear
before her-
For conflict of interest pu{poses, the Commission has "pierced- through entities,
such as nonprofit corporations, on some occasions based on the nature ofthe relationship
between the entity and person who controls the entity. Generally, members of a nonprofit
organization me not considered to be sources of income to an official unless one, or a
few, of the nonprofit organization's members who are financially affected bythe
governmental decision actually conkol the orgarrization's dwisions. Assrmring no one
member, or only a few members, make the decisions of the chamber, including decisions
regarding emplo5rment and compensation, it is not appropriate to pierce through the
nonprofit co4rorate structure as applid to any single member of its board. (Deadrick
Advice Letter, No. I-03-143.)
For purposes of this letter, we assume that Councilmernber James has no other
economic interest relevant to the goveflrmental decision about which you inquire-
Question Four: Are Ms. James's economic interests directly or indirectly involved
in the governmental decision?
"A person, including business entities, sources of income, and sources of gifu, is
directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when that person, either
directly or by an agent:
(l) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an
application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;


File No. I-04-189
Page No. 4
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject o{ the proceeding concerning the decision
before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the
subject petrson." (Regulation I 8704.1( a)(l)-(z).)
If a person who is an economic interest to the public official is not directly
involved in a governmental decision, the person is deemed to be indirectly involved.
(Regulation I 870a. I @).)
Therefore, if the chamber of commerce were the applicant or the subject of the
decision before the council, the chamber would be directly involvd. However, if a
mernber of the chamber of commerce comes before Councilmember James, the official's
economic interest in the chamberwould be indirectlyinvolved.
With respect to personal finances, regulation 18704.5 provides that a public
official or his or her immediate family is deemed to be directly involved in a
governmental decision which has any financial effect on his or herpersonal finances or
those of his or her immediate family- You statod that the oouncil member's salary is
fixed and will not be affected by decisions of the city council. Assurning no decisions
affect the council memb€r's personal expenses, incomg assets, or liabilities, as well as
those of his or her immediate family the council mernber's personal finances will not
result in a conflict of interest-
Question Five: What is the applicable materiality standard?
"Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a source of
income to a public official, and who is directly involved in a decision before the official,s
agency, is deemed material." (Regulation 18705.3.) The standard for determining
materiality of an economic interest directly involved in the governmental decision is the
"one penny'' rule. If the income of the public official were to increase or desrease by one
penny, the financial effect on the economic interest is deerned material-
In order to determine if an indirect economic interest is material, the Commission
has created a set of standards for nonprofit organizations. (Regulation 18705.3.) For
example, in the case of a relatively small nonprofit entity, where the gross annual receipts
are $100,000 or less, the effect of a decision will be material if:
"(i) The decision will result in an increase or decrease of the entit5l's gross annual
receipts for a fiscal year in the amount of $10,000 or more.
(ii) The decision will cause the entity to incur or ayoid additional expenses or to
reduce or eliminate existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or
more-


FileNo. I-04-189
Page No. 5
(iii) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value of the entity's
assets or liabilities in the amount of $10,000 or more." (Regulation
r 8705.3(b)(2XF).)
Thus, if a mernber of the chamber of commerce comes before the city council and
it is reasonably foreseeable that Ms. James's decision would affect the income of the
chamber of commerce, the effect will be considered material if it meets the thresholds in
regulation 18705.3.3
Notwithstanding the materiality standard above, a separate standard can also
define when a public official's governmental decisions will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect upon a source of income that is one of his orher economic
interests. This is the "nexus test," which states "[a]ny reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on a person who is a source of income to a public official is deemd material if the
public official receives or is promised the income to achieve a goal or purpose which
would be achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by the decision." (Regulation
1870s.3(c).)
The rationale for the "nexus" test is that apublic official maynot acoomplish in
his or her public capacity what the public official is paid to accomplish in his or her
private capacity. When a connection exists between a person's job and his or her role as
a public official, there is a presumption that the value of the employee's services to the
employer is based on the fact that the employee is apublic official. In this instance,
Councilmember James would earn a salary as the executive director of the chamber of
cornmerce, thus a nexus may exist in connection with some govemmental decisions. In
order to further analyze potential nexus issues, however, we would need to receive
additional facts regarding a particular governmental decision.
Question Six: Is it substantially likety the governmental decision will meet the
materiality standards?
A material financial effect is "reasonably foreseeable" when it is "substantially
likely'' to occur- This is clarified at regulation 18706(a) to mean "that one or more of the
materiality standards [citations omitted] applicable to that economic interest will be met
as a result of the governmental decision."'When the chamber of commerce is the
applicant before the city council, it is reasonably foreseeable that the materiality
standards would be met.
If a mernber of the chamber of commerce Gomes before the city cormcil,
Councilmember Jamas would have to determine whether she would neod to disqualiff
herself from the decision. Based upon the standards set forth in regulation
18705.3(b)(2XF), she will need to determine if it would be substantially likely that her
decision will have a material financial effect on the chamber of commerce, in which case
3 To better determine if the governmental decision would have a material financial effect we will
need facts specific to that decision, in which case you could request additional advice letters with specific
facts to that decision.


File No. I-04-189
Page No. 6
she will be required to disqualifu herself from the govemmental decision. This would
need to be done on a case by case basis each time a member of the chamber of commerce
comes before the city council as the applicant. You have not given us enough facts to
fi:Jly analyze this question.
Question Seven: WiIl the governmental decision have a financial effect on the
members of the chamber of commerce distinguishable from the public generally?
A public official is not disqualified from a decision despite a conflict of interest if
"the governmental decision affects the public official's eoonomic interests in a manner
which is indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public
generally . . .." (Regulation 18707(a).) You have not provided any facts to indicate that
the exception may applr therefore we have not fully analyzdthis exception.
Question Eight Even if you are disquatified, can your participation be legally
required?
This is anoth€r exception to the disqualificatiorl which applies when the official's
participation is legallyrequired. (Regulation 18708.) Tpically, this involves a situation
in which the agency is unable to assemble a quonrm without the official's participation.
This is a narrowly construed exception that is fact-driven- You provide no facts to
indicate that this situation could occur-
I trust that this letter has addressed CouncilmemberJames's conoerns. Should
you have any further questions regarding this letter, please oontact the Commission at
(916) 322-s660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Cormsel
By: KmaMazzvcca
Intern, Legal Division

of Commerce, will she be disqualified from participating in future land use and business
decisions regarding land and businesses owned by mernbers of the chamber of
commerce?

CONCLUSION: Not necessarily. If Councilmember James were to be employed by the local
chamber of commerce as executive director, the conflict-oFinterest provisions of the Act
require her disqualification in future land use and business decisions regarding Iand and
businesses owned by members of the chamber of commerce only if there was a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the chamber as a result or decision, or
if the decision had a personal financial effect on the council mernber.

formal written advice. (Section 831 14; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)
